audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman who works at an employment agency talking to a man about jobs he could apply for.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, thanks for coming in to see us here at the agency, Joe.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I'm one of the agency representatives, and my name's Sally Baker.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Hi, Sally.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I think we spoke on the phone, didn't we?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: That's right, we did.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: So, thank you for sending in your cv.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: We've had quite a careful look at it, and I think we have two jobs that might be suitable for you.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: The first one is in a company based in North London.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: They're looking for an administrative assistant.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: OK, what sort of company is it?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Uh. They're called Home Solutions.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And they design and make furniture.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Oh, I don't know much about that, but it sounds interesting.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Yes, well as I said, they want someone in their office, and looking at your past experience.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: It does look as if you fit quite a few of the requirements.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: So on your cv, it appears you've done some data entry.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: So that's one skill they want.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Then they expect the person they appoint to attend meetings and take notes there.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: OK, I've done that before, yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: And you'd need to be able to cope with general admin.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Filing, and keeping records and so on? That should be OK.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: And in my last job, I also had to manage the diary.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Excellent.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That's something they want here too.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I'd suggest you add it to your cv.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I don't think you mentioned that, did you?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: So as far as the requirements go, they want good computer skills of course.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And they particularly mention spreadsheets.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That should be fine.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: And interpersonal skills, which would be something they'd check with your references.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I think that should be OK, yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Hmm, and then they mention that they want someone who is careful and takes care with details.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Just looking at your cv, I'd say you're probably all right there.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I think so, yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Do they want any special experience?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I think they wanted some experience of teleconferencing.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Oh, I've got 3 years experience of that.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Uh huh, let's see.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Yes, good.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: In fact they're only asking for at least one year, so that's great.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: So is that something that might interest you?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: It is, yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: The only thing is you said they were in North London, so it would be quite a long commute for me.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So the second position might suit you better, as far as the location goes.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Uh. That's for a warehouse assistant, and that's in South London.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Yes, that would be a lot closer.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: And you've worked in a warehouse before, haven't you?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: So, as far as the responsibilities for this position go, they want someone who can manage the stock, obviously.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And also deliveries.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That should be OK.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: You've got to keep track of stuff, but I've always been quite good with numbers.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That's their first requirement, and they want someone who's computer literate, which we know you are.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Uh. Then they mention organizational skills.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: They want someone who's well organized.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Yes, uh, I think I am.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: And tidy?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Yes, they go together really, don't they?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Then the usual stuff, they want someone who can communicate well both orally and in writing.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: OK, and for the last warehouse job I had, one of the things I enjoyed most was being part of a team.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I found that was really essential for the job.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Excellent.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Yes, they do mention that they want someone who's used to that, yes.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Now, when you were working in a warehouse last time, what sorts of items were you dealing with?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: It was mostly bathroom and kitchen equipment, sinks and stoves and fridges.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: So you're OK moving heavy things?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I'm quite strong, and I've had the training.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Now, as far as experience goes, they mention they want someone with a license, and that you have experience of driving in London.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: So you can cope with the traffic and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Yes, no problem.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: And you've got experience of warehouse work. And the final thing they mention is customer service, I think looking at your cv, you're OK there.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: So what about pay?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Can you tell me a bit more about that please?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Employment Agency: Possible Jobs

audioscript.text: Part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman called Alice Riches talking on the radio about a scheme which involves closing streets to traffic to allow children to play.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: My guest on the show today is Alice Riches, who started the street play scheme where she lives in Beechwood Road.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: For those of you that don't already know, street play involves local residents closing off their street for a few hours so that children have a chance to play in the street safely.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: She started it in her own street, Beechwood Road, and the idea caught on, and there are now street play schemes all over the city.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: So when did you actually start the scheme, Alice?
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: Well, I first had the idea when my oldest child was still a toddler.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: So that's about oh, 6 years ago now, but it took at least 2 years of campaigning before we were actually able to make it happen.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: So the scheme's been up and running for 3 years now.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: We'd love to be able to close our road for longer, for the whole weekend from Saturday morning until Sunday evening for example.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: At the moment, it's just once a week, but when we started, it was only once a month, but we're working on it.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: So what actually happens when Beechwood Road is closed?
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: We have volunteer wardens.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Mostly parents, but some elderly residents too, who block off our road at either end.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: The council have provided special signs, but there's always a volunteer there to explain what's happening to any motorists.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Generally they're fine about it.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: We've only had to get the police involved once or twice.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Now, I should explain that the road isn't completely closed to cars.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: But only residents' cars are allowed.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: If people really need to get in or out of Beechwood Road, it's not a problem, as long as they drive at under 20 km per hour.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: But most people just decide not to use their cars during this time, or they park in another street.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: The wardens are only there to stop through traffic.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: So, can anyone apply to get involved in street play?
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: We want to include all kids in the city, especially those who live on busy roads.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: It's here that demand is greatest, obviously, there isn't such demand in wealthier areas where the children have access to parks or large gardens.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Or in the suburbs where there are usually more places for children to play outside.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I'd recommend that anyone listening who likes the idea should just give it a go.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: We've been surprised by the positive reaction of residents all over the city.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: And that's not just parents, there are always a few who complain, but they're a tiny minority.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: On the whole, everyone is very supportive, and says they're very happy to see children out on the street.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Even if it does get quite noisy.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: There have been so many benefits of street play for the kids.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Parents really like the fact that the kids are getting fresh air, instead of sitting staring at a computer screen.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Even if they're not doing anything particularly energetic, and of course it's great that kids can play with their friends outside without being supervised by their parents.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: But for me, the biggest advantage is that kids develop confidence in themselves to be outside without their parents.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: The other really fantastic thing is that children get to know the adults in the street.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: It's like having a big extended family.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: It certainly does have a lot of benefits.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: I want to move on now and ask you about a related project in King Street.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Well, uh, this was an experiment I was involved in, where local residents decided to try and reduce the traffic along King Street, which is the busiest main road in our area.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: By persuading people not to use their cars for one day.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: We thought about making people pay more for parking, but we decided that would be really unpopular.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: So instead, we just stopped people from parking on King Street, but left the other car parks open.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: It was surprising how much of a difference all this made.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: As we'd predicted, air quality was significantly better, but what I hadn't expected was how much quieter it would be, even with the buses still running.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Of course, everyone said they felt safer, but we were actually amazed that sales in the shops went up considerably that day.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: We thought there'd be fewer people out shopping, not more.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: That's really interesting, so the fact that the street was...
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: But only residents' cars are allowed.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: If people really need to get in or out of Beechwood Road, it's not a problem, as long as they drive at under 20 km per hour.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: But most people just decide not to use their cars during this time, or they park in another street.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: The wardens are only there to stop through traffic.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: So, can anyone apply to get involved in street play?
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: Absolutely.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: We want to include all kids in the city, especially those who live on busy roads.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: It's here that demand is greatest, obviously, there isn't such demand in wealthier areas where the children have access to parks or large gardens.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Or in the suburbs where there are usually more places for children to play outside.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: I'd recommend that anyone listening who likes the idea should just give it a go.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: We've been surprised by the positive reaction of residents all over the city.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: And that's not just parents, there are always a few who complain, but they're a tiny minority.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: On the whole, everyone is very supportive, and says they're very happy to see children out on the street.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Even if it does get quite noisy.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: There have been so many benefits of street play for the kids.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Parents really like the fact that the kids are getting fresh air, instead of sitting staring at a computer screen.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Even if they're not doing anything particularly energetic, and of course it's great that kids can play with their friends outside without being supervised by their parents.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: But for me, the biggest advantage is that kids develop confidence in themselves to be outside without their parents.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: The other really fantastic thing is that children get to know the adults in the street.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: It's like having a big extended family.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: It certainly does have a lot of benefits.
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: I want to move on now and ask you about a related project in King Street.
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: Right.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Well, uh, this was an experiment I was involved in, where local residents decided to try and reduce the traffic along King Street, which is the busiest main road in our area.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: By persuading people not to use their cars for one day.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: We thought about making people pay more for parking, but we decided that would be really unpopular.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: So instead, we just stopped people from parking on King Street, but left the other car parks open.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: It was surprising how much of a difference all this made.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: As we'd predicted, air quality was significantly better, but what I hadn't expected was how much quieter it would be, even with the buses still running.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Of course, everyone said they felt safer, but we were actually amazed that sales in the shops went up considerably that day.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: We thought there'd be fewer people out shopping, not more.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: That's really interesting, so the fact that the street was...
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: When did the Street Play Scheme first take place?
How often is Beechwood Road closed to traffic now?
Who is responsible for closing the road?
Residents who want to use their cars
options: have to park in another street.

items.title: Alice says that Street Play Schemes are most needed in
What has been the reaction of residents who are not parents?
options: Many of them were unhappy at first.
They like seeing children play in the street.
They are surprised by the lack of noise.

body.title: Which TWO benefits for children does Alice think are the most important?
options: opportunity to be part of a community

body.title: Which TWO results of the King Street experiment surprised Alice?

audioscript.text: Part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two students called Hazel and Tom talking about Hazel's media studies assignment.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Tom, could I ask you for some advice, please?
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: If you think I can help.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: What's it about?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: It's my first media studies assignment, and I'm not sure how to go about it.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: You must have done it last year.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Is that the one comparing the coverage of a particular story in a range of newspapers?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Oh yes, I really enjoyed writing it.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: So, what sort of things do I need to compare?
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Well, there are several things.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: For example, there's the question of which page of the newspaper the item appears on.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: You mean because there's a big difference between having it on the front page and the bottom of page 10 for instance?
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: And that shows how important the editor thinks the story is.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Then there's the size, how many column inches the story is given, how many columns it spreads over.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: And I suppose that includes the headline.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: It certainly does.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: It's all part of attracting the reader's attention.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: What about graphics?
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Whether there's anything visual in addition to the text?
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Yes, you need to consider those too.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Because they can have a big effect on the reader's understanding of the story, sometimes a bigger effect than the text itself.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Then you'll need to look at how the item is put together.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: What structure is it given?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Bear in mind that not many people read beyond the first paragraph, so what has the journalist put at the beginning?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: And if say there are conflicting opinions about something, does one appear near the end, where people probably won't read it?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: And newspapers sometimes give wrong or misleading information, don't they?
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Either deliberately or by accident, should I be looking at that too?
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Yes, if you can, compare what's in different versions, and as far as possible.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Try and work out what's true and what isn't, and that relates to a very important point.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: What's the writer's purpose?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Or at least the most important one, if they have several.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: It may seem to be to inform the public, but often it's that they want to create fear.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Or controversy, or to make somebody look ridiculous.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Gosh, I see what you mean.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: And I suppose the writer may make assumptions about the reader.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: That's right, about their knowledge of the subject, their attitudes, and their level of education, which means writing so that the readers understand without feeling patronized.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: All of that will make a difference to how the story is presented.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Does it matter what type of story I write about?
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: No, national or international politics, the arts, anything.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: As long as it's covered in two or three newspapers, though of course it'll be easier and more fun if it's something you're interested in, and know something about.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: And on that basis, a national news item would be worth analyzing.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: I'm quite keen on politics, so I'll try and find a suitable topic.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: What did you choose for your analysis, Tom?
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: I was interested in how newspapers express their opinions explicitly.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: So, I wanted to compare editorials in different papers, but when I started looking, I couldn't find two on the same topic that I felt like analyzing.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: In that case, I wouldn't even bother to look.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: So in the end, I chose a human interest story, a terribly emotional story about a young girl who was very ill.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: And lots of other people, mostly strangers, raised money so she could go abroad for treatment.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Actually, I was surprised.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Some papers just wrote about how wonderful everyone was.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: But others considered the broader picture, like why treatment wasn't available here.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Hmm, I usually find stories like that raise quite strong feelings in me.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: I'll avoid that.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Perhaps I'll choose an arts topic, like different reviews of a film or something about funding for the arts.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: I'll think about that.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: Yes, that might be interesting.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: OK, well, thanks a lot for your help, Tom.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: It's been really useful.
audioscript.speaker: HAZEL

audioscript.text: You're welcome.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Good luck with the assignment, Hazel.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Types of articles

audioscript.text: Part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture on the early history of keeping clean.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Nowadays, we use different products for personal cleanliness, laundry, dishwashing, and household cleaning.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But this is very much a 20th-century development.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The origins of cleanliness date back to prehistoric times.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Since water is essential for life, the earliest people lived near water and knew something about its cleansing properties.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At least that it rinsed MUD off their hands.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: During the excavation of ancient Babylon, evidence was found that soap making was known.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As early as 2800 BC, archaeologists discovered cylinders made of clay with inscriptions on them saying that fats were boiled with ashes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is a method of making soap, though there's no reference to the purpose of this material.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The early Greeks bathed for aesthetic reasons, and apparently didn't use soap.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead, they cleaned their bodies with blocks of sand, pumice, and ashes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then anointed themselves with oil, and scraped off the oil and dirt with a metal instrument known as a strigil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They also used oil mixed with ashes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Clothes were washed without soap in streams.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The ancient Germans and Gauls are also credited with discovering how to make a substance called soap, made of melted animal fat and ashes. They used this mixture to tint their hair red.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Soap got its name according to an ancient Roman legend from Mount Sapo, where animals were sacrificed, leaving deposits of animal fat.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Rain washed these deposits along with wood ashes down into the clay soil along the river Tiber.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Women found that this mixture greatly reduced the effort required to wash their clothes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As Roman civilization advanced, so did bathing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The first of the famous Roman baths, supplied with water from their aqueducts, was built around 312 BC.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The baths were luxurious, and bathing became very popular.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And by the second century AD,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: the Greek physician Galen recommended soap for both medicinal and cleansing purposes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After the fall of Rome in 467 AD, and the resulting decline in bathing habits, much of Europe felt the impact of filth on public health.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This lack of personal cleanliness and related unsanitary living conditions were major factors in the outbreaks of disease in the Middle Ages.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And especially the Black Death of the 14th century.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Nevertheless, soap making became an established craft in Europe, and associations of soap makers guarded their trade secrets closely.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Vegetable and animal oils were used with ashes of plants, along with perfume, apparently for the first time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Gradually, more varieties of soap became available for shaving and shampooing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as bathing and laundering.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A major step toward large scale commercial soap making occurred in 1791 when a French chemist Nicholas LeBlanc patented a process for turning salt into soda ash, or sodium carbonate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Soda ash is the alkali obtained from ashes that combines with fat to form soap.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The LeBlanc process yielded quantities of good quality inexpensive soda ash.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Modern soap making was born some 20 years later, in the early 19th century, with the discovery by Michel Eugène Chevreul, another French chemist, of the chemical nature and relationship of fats, glycerin, and fatty acids.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: His studies established the basis for both fat and soap chemistry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And soap making became a science.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Further developments during the 19th century made it easier and cheaper to manufacture soap.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Until the 19th century, soap was regarded as a luxury item, and was heavily taxed in several countries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As it became more readily available, it became an everyday necessity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A development that was reinforced when the high tax was removed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Soap was then something ordinary people could afford, and cleanliness standards improved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With this widespread use came the development of milder soaps for bathing, and soaps for use in the washing machines that were available to consumers by the turn of the 20th century.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is a method of making soap, though there's no reference to the purpose of this material.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The early Greeks bathed for aesthetic reasons, and apparently didn't use soap.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Instead, they cleaned their bodies with blocks of sand, pumice, and ashes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then anointed themselves with oil, and scraped off the oil and dirt with a metal instrument known as a strigil.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They also used oil mixed with ashes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Clothes were washed without soap in streams.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The ancient Germans and Gauls are also credited with discovering how to make a substance called soap, made of melted animal fat and ashes. They used this mixture to tint their hair red.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Soap got its name according to an ancient Roman legend from Mount Sapo, where animals were sacrificed, leaving deposits of animal fat.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Rain washed these deposits along with wood ashes down into the clay soil along the river Tiber.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Women found that this mixture greatly reduced the effort required to wash their clothes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As Roman civilization advanced, so did bathing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The first of the famous Roman baths, supplied with water from their aqueducts, was built around 312 BC.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The baths were luxurious, and bathing became very popular.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And by the second century AD,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: the Greek physician Galen recommended soap for both medicinal and cleansing purposes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After the fall of Rome in 467 AD, and the resulting decline in bathing habits, much of Europe felt the impact of filth on public health.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This lack of personal cleanliness and related unsanitary living conditions were major factors in the outbreaks of disease in the Middle Ages.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And especially the Black Death of the 14th century.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Nevertheless, soap making became an established craft in Europe, and associations of soap makers guarded their trade secrets closely.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Vegetable and animal oils were used with ashes of plants, along with perfume, apparently for the first time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Gradually, more varieties of soap became available for shaving and shampooing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as bathing and laundering.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A major step toward large scale commercial soap making occurred in 1791 when a French chemist Nicholas LeBlanc patented a process for turning salt into soda ash, or sodium carbonate.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Soda ash is the alkali obtained from ashes that combines with fat to form soap.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The LeBlanc process yielded quantities of good quality inexpensive soda ash.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Modern soap making was born some 20 years later, in the early 19th century, with the discovery by Michel Eugène Chevreul, another French chemist, of the chemical nature and relationship of fats, glycerin, and fatty acids.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: His studies established the basis for both fat and soap chemistry.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And soap making became a science.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Further developments during the 19th century made it easier and cheaper to manufacture soap.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Until the 19th century, soap was regarded as a luxury item, and was heavily taxed in several countries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As it became more readily available, it became an everyday necessity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A development that was reinforced when the high tax was removed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Soap was then something ordinary people could afford, and cleanliness standards improved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: With this widespread use came the development of milder soaps for bathing, and soaps for use in the washing machines that were available to consumers by the turn of the 20th century.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
